text: The first series of Robot Assisted Radical Cystectomy or RARC was reported in two thousand three, the techniques for which continue to evolve. In most of the published series, the robot has been docked in between the legs for RARC.
A side dock with the patient in lithotomy position, however, would allow free perineal access to perform a simultaneous urethrectomy while a sister prostatectomy is being done robotically.

Our index case is a sixty-one-year-old male patient with a past history of right robotic radical nephroureterectomy for a high-grade renal pelvic tumour. He underwent TURBT twice for bladder recurrences followed by induction and maintenance BCG.
Failing BCG, the patient was offered robotic radical cystoprostatectomy with simultaneous urethrectomy due to the involvement of prostatic stroma.
A CECT indicated multiple large polypoidal soft tissue enhancing lesions throughout the urinary bladder.
The patient was taken up for a robotic radical cystoprostatectomy with a simultaneous urethrectomy.
The diversion of choice was a cutaneous ureterostomy in view of the polychronotropic nature of the disease. This would allow easier surveillance in a solitary renal unit.
The surgeon inserts six standard ports for robotic-assisted radical cystectomy, preparing the surgical field for the procedure.
A side dock for RARC would throw up challenges in the form of difficult contralateral lymphadenectomy and varied angulation for pedicular handling.
However, the added advantage of an optimal surgical duration due to a simultaneous urethrectomy encouraged us to perform this surgery via a side dock.
The procedure.
The surgeon begins dissection on the left side, incising the posterior peritoneum to access the virgin plane.
The surgeon identifies the ureter as it crosses over the iliac vessels and loops it for further manipulation.
The surgeon begins the left side lymphadenectomy using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, adhering to the cystectomy template.
The obturator vessels and nerves are exposed and clearly visible alongside the iliac vessels, with the bladder serving as the medial boundary in the surgical field.
The surgeon turns attention to the right side, replicating steps from the previous nephroureterectomy. The right lymphadenectomy is completed, with visible dissection and removal of lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon begins posterior dissection to mobilize the bladder and base of the prostate away from the rectum, with the option to use a thirty-degree up scope for enhanced visualization.
The surgeon clips and divides the obliterated umbilical artery on the right side, using two clips to secure the vessel before cutting between them.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection, carefully approaching the apex of the prostate, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips and divides the obliterated umbilical artery on the left side, similar to the procedure performed on the right side.

The surgeon delineates the right posterior lateral pedicle, applying clips to secure it before dividing the structure. A stapler may also be used to prevent potential hematoma formation post-operatively.
The surgeon makes lateral incisions in the peritoneum to develop the perivesical space, with the incision line running parallel to the visible spermatic cord.
The surgeon develops the perivesical space distally, reaching the endopelvic fascia, which is then incised to continue the dissection.
The surgeon clips and divides the left posterior lateral pedicle, ensuring secure separation of the structure.
The surgeon clips the left ureter as distally as possible, divides it, and sends the specimen for frozen section analysis.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum anteriorly, gaining access to the space of Retzius, which is visible as the surgical field opens up.
The surgeon drops the bladder, incises the endopelvic fascia, and secures the prostatic pedicles, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon actively divides the dorsal venous complex (DVC) under pneumoperitoneum and ligates it using a V-lock suture, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the urethra along its entire circumference, peeling the sphincter away from the urethra to separate these structures.
The perineal surgeon completes the urethral dissection, preparing the urethra for delivery. The distal end of the urethra is stitched and ligated to prevent spillage during the procedure.
The surgeon approximates the levator ani muscle to prevent bowel herniation, while ensuring the mop placed by the perineal surgeon remains in position to prevent leakage of pneumoperitoneum.
The surgeon places the prostatectomy specimen into a surgical bag, clips it securely, and blocks it for removal.
The surgeon dissects the solitary left ureteral unit proximally to gain adequate length, then brings it out as a ureterostomy using the left robotic arm positioned at the stoma site.
The surgeon ensures hemostasis in the surgical field and places a surgical drain to manage fluid accumulation post-procedure.
The surgeon creates a flap cutaneous ureterostomy, visually forming a stoma site for the ureter to exit through the skin.
The total console time was four hours with an estimated blood loss of five hundred ml.
The patient was discharged on day five.
The histopathology showed high-grade invasive papillary urethelial carcinoma

To conclude, The side dock was as effective and dexterous as the conventional central dock. It provides free perineal access with the advantage of allowing simultaneous urethrectomy and hence reducing surgical duration and associate morbidity.
